NAME: FADD.S

============================================================
For RISC-V, please extract all possible information about FADD.S instruction from the text below. The information must be only about FADD.S instruction, not other things. Extract ONLY explicit, testable, normative description about architectural behavior.

Please output in paragraphs. If you want, you can include bullet or numbered points in your output. You can also include tables. However, do not give me any introduction in your output. You just need to output the information that you extract.

If your output includes some sentences that refer to some tables or figures, include those tables or figures too.

Remember, output must be ASCII-only text.

If it happens to be there is no information about FADD.S instruction from the text below, output NONE

The text below:

## 20.6. Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

Floating-point arithmetic instructions with one or two source operands use the R-type format with the OP-FP  major  opcode.  FADD.S  and  FMUL.S  perform  single-precision  floating-point  addition  and multiplication respectively, between rs1 and rs2 .  FSUB.S performs the single-precision floating-point subtraction of rs2 from rs1 .  FDIV.S performs the single-precision floating-point division of rs1 by rs2 . FSQRT.S computes the square root of rs1 . In each case, the result is written to rd .

The 2-bit floating-point format field fmt is  encoded as shown in Table 27. It is set to S (00)  for  all instructions in the F extension.

Table 27. Format field encoding

|   fmt field | Mnemonic   | Meaning                 |
|-------------|------------|-------------------------|
|          00 | S          | 32-bit single-precision |
|          01 | D          | 64-bit double-precision |
|          10 | H          | 16-bit half-precision   |
|          11 | Q          | 128-bit quad-precision  |

All floating-point operations that perform rounding can select the rounding mode using the rm field with the encoding shown in Table 25.

Floating-point  minimum-number  and  maximum-number  instructions  FMIN.S  and  FMAX.S  write, respectively, the smaller or larger of rs1 and rs2 to rd . For the purposes of these instructions only, the value is  considered  to  be  less  than  the  value .  If  both  inputs  are  NaNs,  the  result  is  the canonical NaN. If only one operand is a NaN, the result is the non-NaN operand. Signaling NaN inputs set the invalid operation exception flag, even when the result is not NaN.

<image>

Note that in version 2.2 of the F extension, the FMIN.S and FMAX.S instructions were amended to implement the proposed IEEE 754-201x minimumNumber and maximumNumber operations, rather than the IEEE 754-2008 minNum and maxNum

operations. These operations differ in their handling of signaling NaNs.

<image>

| 31        | 24 25 26 27   | 20   | 19   | 14 15   | 11 12   | 7    | 0      |
|-----------|---------------|------|------|---------|---------|------|--------|
| funct5    | funct5        | fmt  | rs2  | rm rs1  |         | rd   | opcode |
| 5         | 2             | 5    | 5    | 3       |         | 5    | 7      |
| FADD/FSUB | S             | src2 | src1 | RM      |         | dest | OP-FP  |
| FMUL/FDIV | S             | src2 | src1 | RM      |         | dest | OP-FP  |
| FSQRT     | S             | 0    | src  | RM      |         | dest | OP-FP  |
| FMIN-MAX  | S             | src2 | src1 | MIN/MAX |         | dest | OP-FP  |

Floating-point  fused  multiply-add  instructions  require  a  new  standard  instruction  format.  R4-type instructions specify three source registers ( rs1 , rs2 , and rs3 ) and a destination register ( rd ). This format is only used by the floating-point fused multiply-add instructions.

FMADD.S multiplies the values in rs1 and rs2 ,  adds the value in rs3 ,  and writes the final result to rd . FMADD.S computes (rs1 rs2) rs3 .

FMSUB.S multiplies the values in rs1 and rs2 , subtracts the value in rs3 , and writes the final result to rd . FMSUB.S computes (rs1 rs2) rs3 .

FNMSUB.S multiplies the values in rs1 and rs2 , negates the product, adds the value in rs3 , and writes the final result to rd . FNMSUB.S computes -(rs1 rs2) rs3 .

FNMADD.S multiplies the values in rs1 and rs2 ,  negates the product, subtracts the value in rs3 ,  and writes the final result to rd . FNMADD.S computes -(rs1 rs2) rs3 .

<image>

The FNMSUB and FNMADD instructions are counterintuitively named, owing to the naming of the  corresponding  instructions  in  MIPS-IV.  The  MIPS  instructions  were defined  to  negate  the  sum,  rather  than  negating  the  product  as  the  RISC-V instructions  do,  so  the  naming  scheme  was  more  rational  at  the  time.  The  two definitions differ with respect to signed-zero results. The RISC-V definition matches the behavior of the x86 and ARM fused multiply-add instructions, but unfortunately the RISC-V FNMSUB and FNMADD instruction names are swapped as compared to x86, whereas the RISC-V FMSUB and FNMSUB instruction names are swapped as compared to ARM.

<image>

<image>

The  fused  multiply-add  (FMA)  instructions  consume  a  large  part  of  the  32-bit instruction  encoding  space.  Some  alternatives  considered  were  to  restrict  FMA  to only use dynamic rounding modes, but static rounding modes are useful in code that exploits the lack of product rounding. Another alternative would have been to use rd to provide rs3, but this would require additional move instructions in some common sequences.  The  current  design  still  leaves  a  large  portion  of  the  32-bit  encoding space open while avoiding having FMA be non-orthogonal.

The  fused multiply-add instructions must  set the invalid operation exception flag  when  the multiplicands are and zero, even when the addend is a quiet NaN.

<image>

The  IEEE  754-2008  standard  permits,  but  does  not  require,  raising  the  invalid

exception for the operation qNaN.
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
**Operation**  
- `FADD.S` performs single‑precision floating‑point addition of the values in registers `rs1` and `rs2`. The result is written to register `rd`.

**Encoding (R‑type, OP‑FP major opcode)**  

| Field | Meaning for `FADD.S` |
|-------|----------------------|
| `opcode` | `OP‑FP` (the floating‑point major opcode) |
| `fmt`   | `S` (binary 00) – selects 32‑bit single‑precision |
| `rs1`   | First source register (addend) |
| `rs2`   | Second source register (addend) |
| `rm`    | Rounding‑mode field; selects the rounding mode according to Table 25 |
| `rd`    | Destination register (receives the sum) |
| `funct5`| Encoding that distinguishes `FADD.S` from other floating‑point operations (shared with `FSUB.S`) |

**Rounding mode**  
- All floating‑point operations that perform rounding, including `FADD.S`, may select the rounding mode using the `rm` field (encoding defined in Table 25).

**Format field**  
- The 2‑bit `fmt` field for `FADD.S` is set to `00`, which denotes the single‑precision format `S` (32‑bit) as shown in Table 27.  

**Instruction class**  
- `FADD.S` is one of the single‑precision floating‑point computational instructions defined in section 20.6 of the specification. It uses the standard R‑type format with the `OP‑FP` major opcode.
======================================================= END - 1 ========================================================

## 21.7. Double-Precision Floating-Point Classify Instruction

The double-precision floating-point classify instruction, FCLASS.D, is defined analogously to its singleprecision counterpart, but operates on double-precision operands.

<image>
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
- FCLASS.D is the double‑precision floating‑point classify instruction.  
- It is defined analogously to its single‑precision counterpart (FCLASS.S).  
- It operates on double‑precision operands.

For RISC-V, please extract all possible information about FCLASS.S instruction from the text below. The information must be only about FCLASS.S instruction, not other things. Extract ONLY explicit, testable, normative description about architectural behavior.

If it happens to be there is no information about FCLASS.S instruction from the text below, output NONE

The text below:

## 20.9. Single-Precision Floating-Point Classify Instruction

The  FCLASS.S  instruction  examines  the  value  in  floating-point  register rs1 and  writes  to  integer register rd a 10-bit mask that indicates the class of the floating-point number. The format of the mask is  described  in  Table  29.  The  corresponding  bit  in rd will  be  set  if  the  property  is  true  and  clear otherwise. All other bits in rd are cleared. Note that exactly one bit in rd will be set. FCLASS.S does not set the floating-point exception flags.

<image>

Table 29. Format of result of FCLASS instruction.

|   rd bit | Meaning                             |
|----------|-------------------------------------|
|        0 | rs1 is .                            |
|        1 | rs1 is a negative normal number.    |
|        2 | rs1 is a negative subnormal number. |
|        3 | rs1 is .                            |
|        4 | rs1 is .                            |
|        5 | rs1 is a positive subnormal number. |
|        6 | rs1 is a positive normal number.    |
|        7 | rs1 is .                            |
|        8 | rs1 is a signaling NaN.             |
|        9 | rs1 is a quiet NaN.                 |
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
The **FCLASS.S** instruction examines the value in floating‑point register **rs1** and writes to integer register **rd** a 10‑bit mask that indicates the class of the floating‑point number.

* The mask format is defined in Table 29.  
* The corresponding bit in **rd** is set to 1 if the indicated property of **rs1** is true and cleared to 0 otherwise.  
* All bits of **rd** that are not part of the mask are cleared; consequently exactly one of the mask bits in **rd** will be set after execution.  
* **FCLASS.S** does **not** modify any floating‑point exception flags.

**Table 29.** Format of result of FCLASS.S  

| rd bit | Meaning                              |
|--------|--------------------------------------|
| 0      | rs1 is .                             |
| 1      | rs1 is a negative normal number.    |
| 2      | rs1 is a negative subnormal number. |
| 3      | rs1 is .                             |
| 4      | rs1 is .                             |
| 5      | rs1 is a positive subnormal number. |
| 6      | rs1 is a positive normal number.    |
| 7      | rs1 is .                             |
| 8      | rs1 is a signaling NaN.              |
| 9      | rs1 is a quiet NaN.                  |
======================================================= END - 3 ========================================================

NAME: FCVT.D.L

Please output in paragraphs. If you want, you can include bullet or numbered points in your output. You can also include tables. However, do not give me any introduction in your output. You just need to output the information that you extract.

Remember, output must be ASCII-only text.

If it happens to be there is no information about FCVT.D.L instruction from the text below, output NONE

The text below:

## 21.5. Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

Floating-point-to-integer and integer-to-floating-point conversion instructions are encoded in the OPFP major opcode space. FCVT.W.D or FCVT.L.D converts a double-precision floating-point number in floating-point  register rs1 to  a  signed  32-bit  or  64-bit  integer,  respectively,  in  integer  register rd . FCVT.D.W or FCVT.D.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, in integer register rs1 into  a  double-precision  floating-point  number  in  floating-point  register rd .  FCVT.WU.D,  FCVT.LU.D, FCVT.D.WU,  and  FCVT.D.LU  variants convert to or from unsigned integer values. For  RV64, FCVT.W[U].D sign-extends the 32-bit result. FCVT.L[U].D and FCVT.D.L[U] are RV64-only instructions. The range of valid inputs for FCVT. int .D and the behavior for invalid inputs are the same as for FCVT. int .S.

All  floating-point  to  integer  and  integer  to  floating-point  conversion  instructions  round  according  to the rm field. Note FCVT.D.W[U] always produces an exact result and is unaffected by rounding mode.

<image>

The double-precision to single-precision and single-precision to double-precision conversion instructions,  FCVT.S.D  and  FCVT.D.S,  are  encoded  in  the  OP-FP  major  opcode  space  and  both  the source and destination are floating-point registers. The rs2 field encodes the datatype of the source, and the fmt field encodes the datatype of the destination. FCVT.S.D rounds according to the RM field; FCVT.D.S will never round.

<image>

Floating-point  to  floating-point  sign-injection  instructions,  FSGNJ.D,  FSGNJN.D,  and  FSGNJX.D  are defined analogously to the single-precision sign-injection instruction.

<image>

For  XLEN≥64  only,  instructions  are  provided  to  move  bit  patterns  between  the  floating-point  and integer  registers.  FMV.X.D  moves  the  double-precision  value  in  floating-point  register rs1 to  a

representation in IEEE 754-2008 standard encoding in integer register rd . FMV.D.X moves the doubleprecision  value  encoded  in  IEEE  754-2008  standard  encoding  from  the  integer  register rs1 to  the floating-point register rd .

FMV.X.D  and  FMV.D.X  do  not  modify  the  bits  being  transferred;  in  particular,  the  payloads  of  noncanonical NaNs are preserved.

<image>

<image>

Early versions of the RISC-V ISA had additional instructions to allow RV32 systems to transfer between the upper and lower portions of a 64-bit floating-point register and an integer register.  However,  these  would  be  the  only  instructions  with  partial register writes and would add complexity in implementations with recoded floatingpoint or register renaming, requiring a pipeline read-modify-write sequence. Scaling up  to  handling  quad-precision  for  RV32  and  RV64  would  also  require  additional instructions  if  they  were  to  follow  this  pattern.  The  ISA  was  defined  to  reduce  the number of explicit int-float register moves, by having conversions and comparisons write  results  to  the  appropriate  register  file,  so  we  expect  the  benefit  of  these instructions to be lower than for other ISAs.

We note that for systems that implement a 64-bit floating-point unit including fused multiply-add  support  and  64-bit  floating-point  loads  and  stores,  the  marginal hardware  cost  of  moving  from  a  32-bit  to  a  64-bit  integer  datapath  is  low,  and  a software  ABI  supporting  32-bit  wide  address-space  and  pointers  can  be  used  to avoid growth of static data and dynamic memory traffic.
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
- **Instruction name:** FCVT.D.L  
- **Purpose:** Converts a signed 64‑bit integer held in integer register **rs1** into a double‑precision floating‑point value placed in floating‑point register **rd**.  
- **Availability:** RV64‑only (the instruction exists only when the architectural XLEN ≥ 64).  
- **Encoding space:** Part of the OPFP major opcode space (the same space used for other floating‑point‑to‑integer and integer‑to‑floating‑point conversion instructions).  
- **Rounding behavior:** The conversion is performed according to the rounding mode specified in the **rm** field of the instruction (the current floating‑point rounding mode). Unlike FCVT.D.W, FCVT.D.L is *not* exempt from rounding; it is affected by the rm field.  
- **Unsigned variant:** FCVT.D.LU performs the same conversion but treats the 64‑bit integer operand as an unsigned value.  

* normative description extracted directly from the specification text.
======================================================= END - 4 ========================================================

The text below:

## 21.5. Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

Floating-point-to-integer and integer-to-floating-point conversion instructions are encoded in the OPFP major opcode space. FCVT.W.D or FCVT.L.D converts a double-precision floating-point number in floating-point  register rs1 to  a  signed  32-bit  or  64-bit  integer,  respectively,  in  integer  register rd . FCVT.D.W or FCVT.D.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, in integer register rs1 into  a  double-precision  floating-point  number  in  floating-point  register rd .  FCVT.WU.D,  FCVT.LU.D, FCVT.D.WU,  and  FCVT.D.LU  variants convert to or from unsigned integer values. For  RV64, FCVT.W[U].D sign-extends the 32-bit result. FCVT.L[U].D and FCVT.D.L[U] are RV64-only instructions. The range of valid inputs for FCVT. int .D and the behavior for invalid inputs are the same as for FCVT. int .S.

All  floating-point  to  integer  and  integer  to  floating-point  conversion  instructions  round  according  to the rm field. Note FCVT.D.W[U] always produces an exact result and is unaffected by rounding mode.

<image>

The double-precision to single-precision and single-precision to double-precision conversion instructions,  FCVT.S.D  and  FCVT.D.S,  are  encoded  in  the  OP-FP  major  opcode  space  and  both  the source and destination are floating-point registers. The rs2 field encodes the datatype of the source, and the fmt field encodes the datatype of the destination. FCVT.S.D rounds according to the RM field; FCVT.D.S will never round.

<image>

Floating-point  to  floating-point  sign-injection  instructions,  FSGNJ.D,  FSGNJN.D,  and  FSGNJX.D  are defined analogously to the single-precision sign-injection instruction.

<image>

For  XLEN≥64  only,  instructions  are  provided  to  move  bit  patterns  between  the  floating-point  and integer  registers.  FMV.X.D  moves  the  double-precision  value  in  floating-point  register rs1 to  a

representation in IEEE 754-2008 standard encoding in integer register rd . FMV.D.X moves the doubleprecision  value  encoded  in  IEEE  754-2008  standard  encoding  from  the  integer  register rs1 to  the floating-point register rd .

FMV.X.D  and  FMV.D.X  do  not  modify  the  bits  being  transferred;  in  particular,  the  payloads  of  noncanonical NaNs are preserved.

<image>

<image>

Early versions of the RISC-V ISA had additional instructions to allow RV32 systems to transfer between the upper and lower portions of a 64-bit floating-point register and an integer register.  However,  these  would  be  the  only  instructions  with  partial register writes and would add complexity in implementations with recoded floatingpoint or register renaming, requiring a pipeline read-modify-write sequence. Scaling up  to  handling  quad-precision  for  RV32  and  RV64  would  also  require  additional instructions  if  they  were  to  follow  this  pattern.  The  ISA  was  defined  to  reduce  the number of explicit int-float register moves, by having conversions and comparisons write  results  to  the  appropriate  register  file,  so  we  expect  the  benefit  of  these instructions to be lower than for other ISAs.

We note that for systems that implement a 64-bit floating-point unit including fused multiply-add  support  and  64-bit  floating-point  loads  and  stores,  the  marginal hardware  cost  of  moving  from  a  32-bit  to  a  64-bit  integer  datapath  is  low,  and  a software  ABI  supporting  32-bit  wide  address-space  and  pointers  can  be  used  to avoid growth of static data and dynamic memory traffic.
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
- **Opcode class**: FCVT.D.LU is an integer‑to‑floating‑point conversion instruction encoded in the OPFP major opcode space.

- **Operand types**:  
  - Source: a 64‑bit **unsigned** integer in integer register **rs1**.  
  - Destination: a double‑precision floating‑point value placed in floating‑point register **rd**.

- **RV64‑only**: FCVT.D.LU is defined only for the RV64 architecture; it is not available in RV32.

- **Rounding behavior**: The conversion rounds according to the **rm** (rounding‑mode) field of the instruction. (Only FCVT.D.W[U] is specified as "always exact" and unaffected by rounding; FCVT.D.LU follows the general rule that conversion instructions round per **rm**.)

- **Valid input range and invalid‑input handling**: The range of valid inputs for FCVT.D.LU, and the architectural behavior when the input is outside that range, are the same as for the corresponding single‑precision conversion instruction **FCVT.LU.S** (i.e., the same as for **FCVT.int.D**).  

- **Result type**: The result is a double‑precision floating‑point number encoded per the IEEE 754‑2008 standard.  

- **No sign‑extension**: Since the source integer is unsigned, no sign‑extension of the result applies. (Sign‑extension is mentioned only for signed‑integer conversions such as FCVT.W[U].D.)
======================================================= END - 5 ========================================================

The text below:

## 21.5. Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

Floating-point-to-integer and integer-to-floating-point conversion instructions are encoded in the OPFP major opcode space. FCVT.W.D or FCVT.L.D converts a double-precision floating-point number in floating-point  register rs1 to  a  signed  32-bit  or  64-bit  integer,  respectively,  in  integer  register rd . FCVT.D.W or FCVT.D.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, in integer register rs1 into  a  double-precision  floating-point  number  in  floating-point  register rd .  FCVT.WU.D,  FCVT.LU.D, FCVT.D.WU,  and  FCVT.D.LU  variants convert to or from unsigned integer values. For  RV64, FCVT.W[U].D sign-extends the 32-bit result. FCVT.L[U].D and FCVT.D.L[U] are RV64-only instructions. The range of valid inputs for FCVT. int .D and the behavior for invalid inputs are the same as for FCVT. int .S.

All  floating-point  to  integer  and  integer  to  floating-point  conversion  instructions  round  according  to the rm field. Note FCVT.D.W[U] always produces an exact result and is unaffected by rounding mode.

<image>

The double-precision to single-precision and single-precision to double-precision conversion instructions,  FCVT.S.D  and  FCVT.D.S,  are  encoded  in  the  OP-FP  major  opcode  space  and  both  the source and destination are floating-point registers. The rs2 field encodes the datatype of the source, and the fmt field encodes the datatype of the destination. FCVT.S.D rounds according to the RM field; FCVT.D.S will never round.

<image>

Floating-point  to  floating-point  sign-injection  instructions,  FSGNJ.D,  FSGNJN.D,  and  FSGNJX.D  are defined analogously to the single-precision sign-injection instruction.

<image>

For  XLEN≥64  only,  instructions  are  provided  to  move  bit  patterns  between  the  floating-point  and integer  registers.  FMV.X.D  moves  the  double-precision  value  in  floating-point  register rs1 to  a

representation in IEEE 754-2008 standard encoding in integer register rd . FMV.D.X moves the doubleprecision  value  encoded  in  IEEE  754-2008  standard  encoding  from  the  integer  register rs1 to  the floating-point register rd .

FMV.X.D  and  FMV.D.X  do  not  modify  the  bits  being  transferred;  in  particular,  the  payloads  of  noncanonical NaNs are preserved.

<image>

<image>

Early versions of the RISC-V ISA had additional instructions to allow RV32 systems to transfer between the upper and lower portions of a 64-bit floating-point register and an integer register.  However,  these  would  be  the  only  instructions  with  partial register writes and would add complexity in implementations with recoded floatingpoint or register renaming, requiring a pipeline read-modify-write sequence. Scaling up  to  handling  quad-precision  for  RV32  and  RV64  would  also  require  additional instructions  if  they  were  to  follow  this  pattern.  The  ISA  was  defined  to  reduce  the number of explicit int-float register moves, by having conversions and comparisons write  results  to  the  appropriate  register  file,  so  we  expect  the  benefit  of  these instructions to be lower than for other ISAs.

We note that for systems that implement a 64-bit floating-point unit including fused multiply-add  support  and  64-bit  floating-point  loads  and  stores,  the  marginal hardware  cost  of  moving  from  a  32-bit  to  a  64-bit  integer  datapath  is  low,  and  a software  ABI  supporting  32-bit  wide  address-space  and  pointers  can  be  used  to avoid growth of static data and dynamic memory traffic.
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
- **Encoding**: FCVT.D.S is encoded in the OP‑FP major opcode space. Both its source (rs1) and destination (rd) operands are floating‑point registers. The rs2 field encodes the datatype of the source (single‑precision), and the fmt field encodes the datatype of the destination (double‑precision).

- **Operation**: FCVT.D.S converts a single‑precision floating‑point number held in a floating‑point register to a double‑precision floating‑point number placed in a floating‑point register.

- **Rounding behavior**: FCVT.D.S **never rounds**; the conversion produces an exact result, independent of the rounding‑mode (rm) field. (In contrast, FCVT.S.D rounds according to the RM field.)

- **Result**: The destination register receives the exact double‑precision representation of the source single‑precision value; no rounding, overflow, or underflow adjustments are applied.
======================================================= END - 6 ========================================================
########################################################################################################################
########################################################################################################################
########################################################################################################################

The text below:

## 25.3. Round-to-Integer Instructions

The FROUND.S instruction rounds the single-precision floating-point number in floating-point register rs1 to an integer, according to the rounding mode specified in the instruction's rm field. It then writes that integer, represented as a single-precision floating-point number, to floating-point register rd . Zero and  infinite  inputs  are  copied  to rd unmodified.  Signaling  NaN  inputs  cause  the  invalid  operation exception flag to be set; no other exception flags are set. FROUND.S is encoded like FCVT.S.D, but with rs2 =4.

The FROUNDNX.S instruction is defined similarly, but it also sets the inexact exception flag if the input differs from the rounded result and is not NaN. FROUNDNX.S is encoded like FCVT.S.D, but with rs2 =5.

If the D extension is implemented, FROUND.D and FROUNDNX.D instructions are analogously defined to  operate  on  double-precision  numbers.  They  are  encoded  like  FCVT.D.S,  but  with rs2 =4  and  5, respectively,

If  the  Zfh  extension  is  implemented,  FROUND.H  and  FROUNDNX.H  instructions  are  analogously defined to operate on half-precision numbers. They are encoded like FCVT.H.S, but with rs2 =4 and 5, respectively,

If the Q extension is implemented, FROUND.Q and FROUNDNX.Q instructions are analogously defined to  operate  on  quad-precision  numbers.  They  are  encoded  like  FCVT.Q.S,  but  with rs2 =4  and  5, respectively,

<image>

The FROUNDNX.fmt instructions implement the IEEE 754-2019 roundToIntegralExact  operation,  and  the  FROUND.fmt  instructions  implement  the other operations in the roundToIntegral family.
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
FCVT.D.S is an instruction encoding defined in the RISC‑V ISA.  

- When the **D extension** is implemented, the **FROUND.D** and **FROUNDNX.D** instructions are encoded "like FCVT.D.S", with the **rs2** field set to **4** for FROUND.D and **5** for FROUNDNX.D.  

No other explicit normative description of the architectural behavior of FCVT.D.S is provided in the given text.
======================================================= END - 7 ========================================================

The text below:

## 21.5. Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

Floating-point-to-integer and integer-to-floating-point conversion instructions are encoded in the OPFP major opcode space. FCVT.W.D or FCVT.L.D converts a double-precision floating-point number in floating-point  register rs1 to  a  signed  32-bit  or  64-bit  integer,  respectively,  in  integer  register rd . FCVT.D.W or FCVT.D.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, in integer register rs1 into  a  double-precision  floating-point  number  in  floating-point  register rd .  FCVT.WU.D,  FCVT.LU.D, FCVT.D.WU,  and  FCVT.D.LU  variants convert to or from unsigned integer values. For  RV64, FCVT.W[U].D sign-extends the 32-bit result. FCVT.L[U].D and FCVT.D.L[U] are RV64-only instructions. The range of valid inputs for FCVT. int .D and the behavior for invalid inputs are the same as for FCVT. int .S.

All  floating-point  to  integer  and  integer  to  floating-point  conversion  instructions  round  according  to the rm field. Note FCVT.D.W[U] always produces an exact result and is unaffected by rounding mode.

<image>

The double-precision to single-precision and single-precision to double-precision conversion instructions,  FCVT.S.D  and  FCVT.D.S,  are  encoded  in  the  OP-FP  major  opcode  space  and  both  the source and destination are floating-point registers. The rs2 field encodes the datatype of the source, and the fmt field encodes the datatype of the destination. FCVT.S.D rounds according to the RM field; FCVT.D.S will never round.

<image>

Floating-point  to  floating-point  sign-injection  instructions,  FSGNJ.D,  FSGNJN.D,  and  FSGNJX.D  are defined analogously to the single-precision sign-injection instruction.

<image>

For  XLEN≥64  only,  instructions  are  provided  to  move  bit  patterns  between  the  floating-point  and integer  registers.  FMV.X.D  moves  the  double-precision  value  in  floating-point  register rs1 to  a

representation in IEEE 754-2008 standard encoding in integer register rd . FMV.D.X moves the doubleprecision  value  encoded  in  IEEE  754-2008  standard  encoding  from  the  integer  register rs1 to  the floating-point register rd .

FMV.X.D  and  FMV.D.X  do  not  modify  the  bits  being  transferred;  in  particular,  the  payloads  of  noncanonical NaNs are preserved.

<image>

<image>

Early versions of the RISC-V ISA had additional instructions to allow RV32 systems to transfer between the upper and lower portions of a 64-bit floating-point register and an integer register.  However,  these  would  be  the  only  instructions  with  partial register writes and would add complexity in implementations with recoded floatingpoint or register renaming, requiring a pipeline read-modify-write sequence. Scaling up  to  handling  quad-precision  for  RV32  and  RV64  would  also  require  additional instructions  if  they  were  to  follow  this  pattern.  The  ISA  was  defined  to  reduce  the number of explicit int-float register moves, by having conversions and comparisons write  results  to  the  appropriate  register  file,  so  we  expect  the  benefit  of  these instructions to be lower than for other ISAs.

We note that for systems that implement a 64-bit floating-point unit including fused multiply-add  support  and  64-bit  floating-point  loads  and  stores,  the  marginal hardware  cost  of  moving  from  a  32-bit  to  a  64-bit  integer  datapath  is  low,  and  a software  ABI  supporting  32-bit  wide  address-space  and  pointers  can  be  used  to avoid growth of static data and dynamic memory traffic.
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
FCVT.D.W is an integer‑to‑floating‑point conversion instruction encoded in the OPFP major opcode space.  

* **Operation** – It converts a signed 32‑bit integer value held in integer register rs1 to a double‑precision (64‑bit) floating‑point value written to floating‑point register rd.  

* **Operand size** – The source integer is a 32‑bit signed value; the destination is a double‑precision floating‑point number. In RV64 implementations the 32‑bit integer in rs1 is sign‑extended to 64 bits before the conversion.  

* **Rounding** – Although all integer‑to‑floating‑point conversion instructions nominally round according to the **rm** field, FCVT.D.W (and its unsigned variant FCVT.D.WU) always produces an exact result and is **unaffected by the rounding mode**.  

* **Validity of inputs** – The set of input values that are considered valid, and the architectural behavior for inputs that are out of range (e.g., overflow, NaN generation, or invalid operation), are exactly the same as for the single‑precision counterpart **FCVT.int.D**.  

* **RV64‑only variants** – The related variant **FCVT.D.L** (converting a signed 64‑bit integer) is RV64‑only; FCVT.D.W itself is defined for both RV32 and RV64.  

* **Unsigned variant** – The unsigned version is **FCVT.D.WU**, which also always yields an exact result and is unaffected by rounding mode.  

* **Encoding summary** – The instruction belongs to the OPFP major opcode class; rs1 encodes the integer source register, rd encodes the floating‑point destination register, and the rm field is present but ignored for FCVT.D.W.
======================================================= END - 8 ========================================================

## 21.5. Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

Floating-point-to-integer and integer-to-floating-point conversion instructions are encoded in the OPFP major opcode space. FCVT.W.D or FCVT.L.D converts a double-precision floating-point number in floating-point  register rs1 to  a  signed  32-bit  or  64-bit  integer,  respectively,  in  integer  register rd . FCVT.D.W or FCVT.D.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, in integer register rs1 into  a  double-precision  floating-point  number  in  floating-point  register rd .  FCVT.WU.D,  FCVT.LU.D, FCVT.D.WU,  and  FCVT.D.LU  variants convert to or from unsigned integer values. For  RV64, FCVT.W[U].D sign-extends the 32-bit result. FCVT.L[U].D and FCVT.D.L[U] are RV64-only instructions. The range of valid inputs for FCVT. int .D and the behavior for invalid inputs are the same as for FCVT. int .S.

All  floating-point  to  integer  and  integer  to  floating-point  conversion  instructions  round  according  to the rm field. Note FCVT.D.W[U] always produces an exact result and is unaffected by rounding mode.

<image>

The double-precision to single-precision and single-precision to double-precision conversion instructions,  FCVT.S.D  and  FCVT.D.S,  are  encoded  in  the  OP-FP  major  opcode  space  and  both  the source and destination are floating-point registers. The rs2 field encodes the datatype of the source, and the fmt field encodes the datatype of the destination. FCVT.S.D rounds according to the RM field; FCVT.D.S will never round.

<image>

Floating-point  to  floating-point  sign-injection  instructions,  FSGNJ.D,  FSGNJN.D,  and  FSGNJX.D  are defined analogously to the single-precision sign-injection instruction.

<image>

For  XLEN≥64  only,  instructions  are  provided  to  move  bit  patterns  between  the  floating-point  and integer  registers.  FMV.X.D  moves  the  double-precision  value  in  floating-point  register rs1 to  a

representation in IEEE 754-2008 standard encoding in integer register rd . FMV.D.X moves the doubleprecision  value  encoded  in  IEEE  754-2008  standard  encoding  from  the  integer  register rs1 to  the floating-point register rd .

FMV.X.D  and  FMV.D.X  do  not  modify  the  bits  being  transferred;  in  particular,  the  payloads  of  noncanonical NaNs are preserved.

<image>

<image>

Early versions of the RISC-V ISA had additional instructions to allow RV32 systems to transfer between the upper and lower portions of a 64-bit floating-point register and an integer register.  However,  these  would  be  the  only  instructions  with  partial register writes and would add complexity in implementations with recoded floatingpoint or register renaming, requiring a pipeline read-modify-write sequence. Scaling up  to  handling  quad-precision  for  RV32  and  RV64  would  also  require  additional instructions  if  they  were  to  follow  this  pattern.  The  ISA  was  defined  to  reduce  the number of explicit int-float register moves, by having conversions and comparisons write  results  to  the  appropriate  register  file,  so  we  expect  the  benefit  of  these instructions to be lower than for other ISAs.

We note that for systems that implement a 64-bit floating-point unit including fused multiply-add  support  and  64-bit  floating-point  loads  and  stores,  the  marginal hardware  cost  of  moving  from  a  32-bit  to  a  64-bit  integer  datapath  is  low,  and  a software  ABI  supporting  32-bit  wide  address-space  and  pointers  can  be  used  to avoid growth of static data and dynamic memory traffic.
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
FCVT.D.WU is an integer‑to‑floating‑point conversion instruction defined in the OPFP major opcode space.

- **Operand types**  
  - **source (rs1):** integer register containing a 32‑bit *unsigned* integer.  
  - **destination (rd):** floating‑point register that receives a double‑precision (64‑bit) floating‑point value.

- **Operation**  
  - Converts the unsigned integer value in *rs1* to its IEEE 754‑2008 double‑precision floating‑point representation and writes the result to *rd*.

- **Rounding behavior**  
  - Although all integer‑to‑floating‑point conversion instructions have an *rm* field, **FCVT.D.WU always produces an exact result and is unaffected by the rounding mode**. The *rm* field is ignored.

- **Valid input range and invalid‑input handling**  
  - The range of values that can be converted and the handling of inputs that are out of range are *identical* to those defined for the generic **FCVT.int.D** conversions (i.e., the same rules that apply to signed‑integer conversions to double precision).

- **Architectural notes**  
  - FCVT.D.WU is one of the "unsigned" variants (FCVT.WU.D, FCVT.LU.D, FCVT.D.WU, FCVT.D.LU) that convert to or from unsigned integer values.  
  - No special sign‑extension or RV‑mode restrictions are mentioned for FCVT.D.WU; the instruction is defined for the base ISA (both RV32 and RV64) unless otherwise limited by the implementation.
======================================================= END - 9 ========================================================

## 21.5. Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

Floating-point-to-integer and integer-to-floating-point conversion instructions are encoded in the OPFP major opcode space. FCVT.W.D or FCVT.L.D converts a double-precision floating-point number in floating-point  register rs1 to  a  signed  32-bit  or  64-bit  integer,  respectively,  in  integer  register rd . FCVT.D.W or FCVT.D.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, in integer register rs1 into  a  double-precision  floating-point  number  in  floating-point  register rd .  FCVT.WU.D,  FCVT.LU.D, FCVT.D.WU,  and  FCVT.D.LU  variants convert to or from unsigned integer values. For  RV64, FCVT.W[U].D sign-extends the 32-bit result. FCVT.L[U].D and FCVT.D.L[U] are RV64-only instructions. The range of valid inputs for FCVT. int .D and the behavior for invalid inputs are the same as for FCVT. int .S.

All  floating-point  to  integer  and  integer  to  floating-point  conversion  instructions  round  according  to the rm field. Note FCVT.D.W[U] always produces an exact result and is unaffected by rounding mode.

<image>

The double-precision to single-precision and single-precision to double-precision conversion instructions,  FCVT.S.D  and  FCVT.D.S,  are  encoded  in  the  OP-FP  major  opcode  space  and  both  the source and destination are floating-point registers. The rs2 field encodes the datatype of the source, and the fmt field encodes the datatype of the destination. FCVT.S.D rounds according to the RM field; FCVT.D.S will never round.

<image>

Floating-point  to  floating-point  sign-injection  instructions,  FSGNJ.D,  FSGNJN.D,  and  FSGNJX.D  are defined analogously to the single-precision sign-injection instruction.

<image>

For  XLEN≥64  only,  instructions  are  provided  to  move  bit  patterns  between  the  floating-point  and integer  registers.  FMV.X.D  moves  the  double-precision  value  in  floating-point  register rs1 to  a

representation in IEEE 754-2008 standard encoding in integer register rd . FMV.D.X moves the doubleprecision  value  encoded  in  IEEE  754-2008  standard  encoding  from  the  integer  register rs1 to  the floating-point register rd .

FMV.X.D  and  FMV.D.X  do  not  modify  the  bits  being  transferred;  in  particular,  the  payloads  of  noncanonical NaNs are preserved.

<image>

<image>

Early versions of the RISC-V ISA had additional instructions to allow RV32 systems to transfer between the upper and lower portions of a 64-bit floating-point register and an integer register.  However,  these  would  be  the  only  instructions  with  partial register writes and would add complexity in implementations with recoded floatingpoint or register renaming, requiring a pipeline read-modify-write sequence. Scaling up  to  handling  quad-precision  for  RV32  and  RV64  would  also  require  additional instructions  if  they  were  to  follow  this  pattern.  The  ISA  was  defined  to  reduce  the number of explicit int-float register moves, by having conversions and comparisons write  results  to  the  appropriate  register  file,  so  we  expect  the  benefit  of  these instructions to be lower than for other ISAs.

We note that for systems that implement a 64-bit floating-point unit including fused multiply-add  support  and  64-bit  floating-point  loads  and  stores,  the  marginal hardware  cost  of  moving  from  a  32-bit  to  a  64-bit  integer  datapath  is  low,  and  a software  ABI  supporting  32-bit  wide  address-space  and  pointers  can  be  used  to avoid growth of static data and dynamic memory traffic.
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
FCVT.L.D is a floating‑point‑to‑integer conversion instruction that resides in the OPFP major opcode space.

* **Operation** – Converts the double‑precision floating‑point value in floating‑point register **rs1** to a **signed 64‑bit integer** and writes the result to integer register **rd**.  

* **Availability** – The instruction is **RV64‑only**; it is not defined for RV32 implementations.  

* **Rounding** – The conversion follows the rounding mode specified in the **rm** field of the instruction.  

* **Input range and invalid input handling** – The set of valid input values and the architectural behavior for inputs outside that range are **identical to those defined for the corresponding single‑precision conversion instruction (FCVT.int.S)**.  

* **Instruction classification** – FCVT.L.D is one of the double‑precision floating‑point conversion instructions that operate between floating‑point registers and the integer register file.
======================================================= END - 10 =======================================================

########################################################################################################################

====================================================== START - 11 ======================================================
GLOBAL NUMBER: 11
LOCAL NUMBER: 1
NAME: FCVT.L.S
============================================================
=======
PROMPT:
=======
============================================================
For RISC-V, please extract all possible information about FCVT.L.S instruction from the text below. The information must be only about FCVT.L.S instruction, not other things. Extract ONLY explicit, testable, normative description about architectural behavior.

Please output in paragraphs. If you want, you can include bullet or numbered points in your output. You can also include tables. However, do not give me any introduction in your output. You just need to output the information that you extract.

If your output includes some sentences that refer to some tables or figures, include those tables or figures too.

Remember, output must be ASCII-only text.

If it happens to be there is no information about FCVT.L.S instruction from the text below, output NONE

The text below:

## 20.7. Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

Floating-point-to-integer and integer-to-floating-point conversion instructions are encoded in the OPFP  major  opcode  space.  FCVT.W.S  or  FCVT.L.S  converts  a  floating-point  number  in  floating-point register rs1 to  a  signed  32-bit  or  64-bit  integer,  respectively,  in  integer  register rd .  FCVT.S.W  or FCVT.S.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, in integer register rs1 into a floatingpoint number in floating-point register rd . FCVT.WU.S, FCVT.LU.S, FCVT.S.WU, and FCVT.S.LU variants convert to or from unsigned integer values. For XLEN ,  FCVT.W[U].S sign-extends the 32-bit result to  the  destination  register  width.  FCVT.L[U].S  and  FCVT.S.L[U]  are  RV64-only  instructions.  If  the rounded result is not representable in the destination format, it is clipped to the nearest value and the invalid flag is set. Table 28 gives the range of valid inputs for FCVT. int .S and the behavior for invalid inputs.

All  floating-point  to  integer  and  integer  to  floating-point  conversion  instructions  round  according  to the rm field. A floating-point register can be initialized to floating-point positive zero using FCVT.S.W rd , x0 , which will never set any exception flags.

Table 28. Domains of float-to-integer conversions and behavior for invalid inputs

|                                        | FCVT.W.S   | FCVT.WU.S   | FCVT.L.S   | FCVT.LU.S   |
|----------------------------------------|------------|-------------|------------|-------------|
| Minimum valid input (after rounding)   |            | 0           |            | 0           |
| Maximum valid input (after rounding)   |            |             |            |             |
| Output for out-of-range negative input |            | 0           |            | 0           |
| Output for                             |            | 0           |            | 0           |
| Output for out-of-range positive input |            |             |            |             |
| Output for or NaN                      |            |             |            |             |

All  floating-point  conversion  instructions  set  the  Inexact  exception  flag  if  the  rounded  result  differs from the operand value and the Invalid exception flag is not set.

<image>

Floating-point  to  floating-point  sign-injection  instructions,  FSGNJ.S,  FSGNJN.S,  and  FSGNJX.S, produce a result that takes all bits except the sign bit from rs1 . For FSGNJ, the result's sign bit is rs2 's sign bit; for FSGNJN, the result's sign bit is the opposite of rs2 's sign bit; and for FSGNJX, the sign bit is  the  XOR  of  the  sign  bits  of rs1 and rs2 .  Sign-injection  instructions  do  not  set  floating-point exception  flags,  nor  do  they  canonicalize  NaNs.  Note,  FSGNJ.S rx,  ry,  ry moves ry to rx (assembler pseudoinstruction  FMV.S rx,  ry );  FSGNJN.S rx,  ry,  ry moves  the  negation  of ry to rx (assembler pseudoinstruction  FNEG.S rx,  ry );  and  FSGNJX.S rx,  ry,  ry moves  the  absolute  value  of ry to rx (assembler pseudoinstruction FABS.S rx, ry ).

<image>

<image>

The sign-injection instructions provide floating-point MV, ABS, and NEG, as well as supporting a few other operations, including the IEEE copySign operation and sign manipulation in transcendental math function libraries. Although MV, ABS, and NEG only  need  a  single  register  operand,  whereas  FSGNJ  instructions  need  two,  it  is unlikely  most  microarchitectures  would  add  optimizations  to  benefit  from  the reduced number of register reads for these relatively infrequent instructions. Even in this case, a microarchitecture can simply detect when both source registers are the same for FSGNJ instructions and only read a single copy.

Instructions  are  provided  to  move  bit  patterns  between  the  floating-point  and  integer  registers. FMV.X.W moves the single-precision value in floating-point register rs1 represented in IEEE 754-2008 encoding to the lower 32 bits of integer register rd .  The bits are not modified in the transfer, and in particular,  the  payloads  of  non-canonical  NaNs  are  preserved.  For  RV64,  the  higher  32  bits  of  the destination register are filled with copies of the floating-point number's sign bit.

FMV.W.X moves the single-precision value encoded in IEEE 754-2008 standard encoding from the lower 32 bits of integer register rs1 to the floating-point register rd .  The bits are not modified in the transfer, and in particular, the payloads of non-canonical NaNs are preserved.

<image>

The FMV.W.X and FMV.X.W instructions were previously called FMV.S.X and FMV.X.S. The use of W is more consistent with their semantics as an instruction that moves 32 bits  without  interpreting  them.  This  became  clearer  after  defining  NaN-boxing.  To avoid disturbing existing code, both the W and S versions will be supported by tools.

<image>

<image>

The base floating-point ISA was defined so as to allow implementations to employ an internal  recoding  of  the  floating-point  format  in  registers  to  simplify  handling  of subnormal values and possibly to reduce functional unit latency. To this end, the F extension  avoids  representing  integer  values  in  the  floating-point  registers  by defining  conversion  and  comparison  operations  that  read  and  write  the  integer register  file  directly.  This  also  removes  many  of  the  common  cases  where  explicit moves between integer and floating-point registers are required, reducing instruction count and critical paths for common mixed-format code sequences.
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
FCVT.L.S converts a single‑precision floating‑point value in floating‑point register **rs1** to a signed 64‑bit integer written to integer register **rd**. The instruction is defined only for the RV64 base ISA (i.e., it is an RV64‑only instruction).

* The conversion obeys the rounding mode specified in the **rm** field of the instruction; all floating‑point‑to‑integer conversion instructions round according to **rm**.

* After rounding, if the integer value is not representable in the signed 64‑bit destination format, the result is clipped to the nearest representable value and the **invalid** exception flag is set.

* If the rounded integer differs from the original floating‑point operand, the **inexact** exception flag is set, unless the invalid flag has already been set.

* Because the destination is a full‑width 64‑bit integer, no sign‑extension is performed (sign‑extension is only mentioned for the 32‑bit result instructions FCVT.W[U].S).

* FCVT.L.S is listed in the "float‑to‑integer" domain table (Table 28). The table rows for this instruction are present but the specific numeric limits are omitted in the excerpt:

```
                | FCVT.L.S |
----------------|----------|
Minimum valid input (after rounding)   |          |
Maximum valid input (after rounding)   |          |
Output for out-of-range negative input |          |
Output for out-of-range positive input |          |
Output for NaN                         |          |
```

* The instruction is part of the OPFP major opcode space along with its signed‑unsigned and 32‑bit counterparts (FCVT.W.S, FCVT.LU.S, etc.).  

These points constitute the explicit, normative architectural behavior of the **FCVT.L.S** instruction as described in the provided text.
======================================================= END - 11 =======================================================
########################################################################################################################
########################################################################################################################
########################################################################################################################
====================================================== START - 12 ======================================================
GLOBAL NUMBER: 12
LOCAL NUMBER: 1
NAME: FCVT.LU.D
============================================================

=======

For RISC-V, please extract all possible information about FCVT.LU.D instruction from the text below. The information must be only about FCVT.LU.D instruction, not other things. Extract ONLY explicit, testable, normative description about architectural behavior.

Please output in paragraphs. If you want, you can include bullet or numbered points in your output. You can also include tables. However, do not give me any introduction in your output. You just need to output the information that you extract.

If your output includes some sentences that refer to some tables or figures, include those tables or figures too.

Remember, output must be ASCII-only text.

If it happens to be there is no information about FCVT.LU.D instruction from the text below, output NONE

The text below:

## 21.5. Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

Floating-point-to-integer and integer-to-floating-point conversion instructions are encoded in the OPFP major opcode space. FCVT.W.D or FCVT.L.D converts a double-precision floating-point number in floating-point  register rs1 to  a  signed  32-bit  or  64-bit  integer,  respectively,  in  integer  register rd . FCVT.D.W or FCVT.D.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, in integer register rs1 into  a  double-precision  floating-point  number  in  floating-point  register rd .  FCVT.WU.D,  FCVT.LU.D, FCVT.D.WU,  and  FCVT.D.LU  variants convert to or from unsigned integer values. For  RV64, FCVT.W[U].D sign-extends the 32-bit result. FCVT.L[U].D and FCVT.D.L[U] are RV64-only instructions. The range of valid inputs for FCVT. int .D and the behavior for invalid inputs are the same as for FCVT. int .S.

All  floating-point  to  integer  and  integer  to  floating-point  conversion  instructions  round  according  to the rm field. Note FCVT.D.W[U] always produces an exact result and is unaffected by rounding mode.

<image>

The double-precision to single-precision and single-precision to double-precision conversion instructions,  FCVT.S.D  and  FCVT.D.S,  are  encoded  in  the  OP-FP  major  opcode  space  and  both  the source and destination are floating-point registers. The rs2 field encodes the datatype of the source, and the fmt field encodes the datatype of the destination. FCVT.S.D rounds according to the RM field; FCVT.D.S will never round.

<image>

Floating-point  to  floating-point  sign-injection  instructions,  FSGNJ.D,  FSGNJN.D,  and  FSGNJX.D  are defined analogously to the single-precision sign-injection instruction.

<image>

For  XLEN≥64  only,  instructions  are  provided  to  move  bit  patterns  between  the  floating-point  and integer  registers.  FMV.X.D  moves  the  double-precision  value  in  floating-point  register rs1 to  a

representation in IEEE 754-2008 standard encoding in integer register rd . FMV.D.X moves the doubleprecision  value  encoded  in  IEEE  754-2008  standard  encoding  from  the  integer  register rs1 to  the floating-point register rd .

FMV.X.D  and  FMV.D.X  do  not  modify  the  bits  being  transferred;  in  particular,  the  payloads  of  noncanonical NaNs are preserved.

<image>

<image>

Early versions of the RISC-V ISA had additional instructions to allow RV32 systems to transfer between the upper and lower portions of a 64-bit floating-point register and an integer register.  However,  these  would  be  the  only  instructions  with  partial register writes and would add complexity in implementations with recoded floatingpoint or register renaming, requiring a pipeline read-modify-write sequence. Scaling up  to  handling  quad-precision  for  RV32  and  RV64  would  also  require  additional instructions  if  they  were  to  follow  this  pattern.  The  ISA  was  defined  to  reduce  the number of explicit int-float register moves, by having conversions and comparisons write  results  to  the  appropriate  register  file,  so  we  expect  the  benefit  of  these instructions to be lower than for other ISAs.

We note that for systems that implement a 64-bit floating-point unit including fused multiply-add  support  and  64-bit  floating-point  loads  and  stores,  the  marginal hardware  cost  of  moving  from  a  32-bit  to  a  64-bit  integer  datapath  is  low,  and  a software  ABI  supporting  32-bit  wide  address-space  and  pointers  can  be  used  to avoid growth of static data and dynamic memory traffic.
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
**FCVT.LU.D – double‑precision floating‑point to unsigned integer conversion (RV64‑only)**  

- **Opcode space**: The FCVT.LU.D instruction is one of the floating‑point‑to‑integer conversion instructions encoded in the **OPFP major opcode space**.  

- **Operand types**:  
  - **Source**: floating‑point register **rs1** holds a **double‑precision (64‑bit) floating‑point number**.  
  - **Destination**: integer register **rd** receives the **unsigned 64‑bit integer** result.  

- **Availability**: The instruction is **RV64‑only**; it does not exist in RV32 implementations.  

- **Rounding behavior**: The conversion **rounds according to the rounding‑mode field (rm)** of the instruction, just as all other floating‑point‑to‑integer conversion instructions do.  

- **Input range and invalid‑input handling**:  
  - The **range of valid inputs** for FCVT.LU.D and the **behaviour for invalid inputs** are **identical to those defined for the FCVT.int.S family** (i.e., the signed‑integer conversion of a double‑precision operand).  
  - Values that are outside the representable unsigned‑64‑bit range, NaNs, infinities, or values that cannot be represented after rounding invoke the same exception and result handling as specified for FCVT.int.S.  

- **Sign‑extension**: The text only specifies that **FCVT.W[U].D sign‑extends the 32‑bit result** on RV64. No sign‑extension is defined for FCVT.LU.D, which produces a full 64‑bit unsigned result.  

- **Effect of rounding mode on exact results**: The note that **FCVT.D.W[U] always produces an exact result and is unaffected by rounding mode** does **not** apply to FCVT.LU.D; therefore, rounding mode can affect the result of FCVT.LU.D when the source value is not exactly representable as an unsigned 64‑bit integer.
======================================================= END - 12 =======================================================
########################################################################################################################
########################################################################################################################
########################################################################################################################

NAME: FCVT.LU.S

Please output in paragraphs. If you want, you can include bullet or numbered points in your output. You can also include tables. However, do not give me any introduction in your output. You just need to output the information that you extract.

If it happens to be there is no information about FCVT.LU.S instruction from the text below, output NONE

The text below:

## 20.7. Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

Floating-point-to-integer and integer-to-floating-point conversion instructions are encoded in the OPFP  major  opcode  space.  FCVT.W.S  or  FCVT.L.S  converts  a  floating-point  number  in  floating-point register rs1 to  a  signed  32-bit  or  64-bit  integer,  respectively,  in  integer  register rd .  FCVT.S.W  or FCVT.S.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, in integer register rs1 into a floatingpoint number in floating-point register rd . FCVT.WU.S, FCVT.LU.S, FCVT.S.WU, and FCVT.S.LU variants convert to or from unsigned integer values. For XLEN ,  FCVT.W[U].S sign-extends the 32-bit result to  the  destination  register  width.  FCVT.L[U].S  and  FCVT.S.L[U]  are  RV64-only  instructions.  If  the rounded result is not representable in the destination format, it is clipped to the nearest value and the invalid flag is set. Table 28 gives the range of valid inputs for FCVT. int .S and the behavior for invalid inputs.

All  floating-point  to  integer  and  integer  to  floating-point  conversion  instructions  round  according  to the rm field. A floating-point register can be initialized to floating-point positive zero using FCVT.S.W rd , x0 , which will never set any exception flags.

Table 28. Domains of float-to-integer conversions and behavior for invalid inputs

|                                        | FCVT.W.S   | FCVT.WU.S   | FCVT.L.S   | FCVT.LU.S   |
|----------------------------------------|------------|-------------|------------|-------------|
| Minimum valid input (after rounding)   |            | 0           |            | 0           |
| Maximum valid input (after rounding)   |            |             |            |             |
| Output for out-of-range negative input |            | 0           |            | 0           |
| Output for                             |            | 0           |            | 0           |
| Output for out-of-range positive input |            |             |            |             |
| Output for or NaN                      |            |             |            |             |

All  floating-point  conversion  instructions  set  the  Inexact  exception  flag  if  the  rounded  result  differs from the operand value and the Invalid exception flag is not set.

<image>

Floating-point  to  floating-point  sign-injection  instructions,  FSGNJ.S,  FSGNJN.S,  and  FSGNJX.S, produce a result that takes all bits except the sign bit from rs1 . For FSGNJ, the result's sign bit is rs2 's sign bit; for FSGNJN, the result's sign bit is the opposite of rs2 's sign bit; and for FSGNJX, the sign bit is  the  XOR  of  the  sign  bits  of rs1 and rs2 .  Sign-injection  instructions  do  not  set  floating-point exception  flags,  nor  do  they  canonicalize  NaNs.  Note,  FSGNJ.S rx,  ry,  ry moves ry to rx (assembler pseudoinstruction  FMV.S rx,  ry );  FSGNJN.S rx,  ry,  ry moves  the  negation  of ry to rx (assembler pseudoinstruction  FNEG.S rx,  ry );  and  FSGNJX.S rx,  ry,  ry moves  the  absolute  value  of ry to rx (assembler pseudoinstruction FABS.S rx, ry ).

<image>

<image>

The sign-injection instructions provide floating-point MV, ABS, and NEG, as well as supporting a few other operations, including the IEEE copySign operation and sign manipulation in transcendental math function libraries. Although MV, ABS, and NEG only  need  a  single  register  operand,  whereas  FSGNJ  instructions  need  two,  it  is unlikely  most  microarchitectures  would  add  optimizations  to  benefit  from  the reduced number of register reads for these relatively infrequent instructions. Even in this case, a microarchitecture can simply detect when both source registers are the same for FSGNJ instructions and only read a single copy.

Instructions  are  provided  to  move  bit  patterns  between  the  floating-point  and  integer  registers. FMV.X.W moves the single-precision value in floating-point register rs1 represented in IEEE 754-2008 encoding to the lower 32 bits of integer register rd .  The bits are not modified in the transfer, and in particular,  the  payloads  of  non-canonical  NaNs  are  preserved.  For  RV64,  the  higher  32  bits  of  the destination register are filled with copies of the floating-point number's sign bit.

FMV.W.X moves the single-precision value encoded in IEEE 754-2008 standard encoding from the lower 32 bits of integer register rs1 to the floating-point register rd .  The bits are not modified in the transfer, and in particular, the payloads of non-canonical NaNs are preserved.

<image>

The FMV.W.X and FMV.X.W instructions were previously called FMV.S.X and FMV.X.S. The use of W is more consistent with their semantics as an instruction that moves 32 bits  without  interpreting  them.  This  became  clearer  after  defining  NaN-boxing.  To avoid disturbing existing code, both the W and S versions will be supported by tools.

<image>

<image>

The base floating-point ISA was defined so as to allow implementations to employ an internal  recoding  of  the  floating-point  format  in  registers  to  simplify  handling  of subnormal values and possibly to reduce functional unit latency. To this end, the F extension  avoids  representing  integer  values  in  the  floating-point  registers  by defining  conversion  and  comparison  operations  that  read  and  write  the  integer register  file  directly.  This  also  removes  many  of  the  common  cases  where  explicit moves between integer and floating-point registers are required, reducing instruction count and critical paths for common mixed-format code sequences.
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
**FCVT.LU.S (floating‑point‑to‑unsigned‑64‑bit‑integer conversion, single‑precision)**  

- **Purpose** – Converts the single‑precision floating‑point value in source floating‑point register `rs1` to an unsigned 64‑bit integer placed in integer register `rd`.  
- **Availability** – RV64‑only instruction (the `FCVT.L[U].S` family is defined only for RV64).  
- **Encoding space** – Belongs to the OPFP major opcode space (the floating‑point‑to‑integer conversion opcode group).  
- **Rounding** – The conversion rounds according to the `rm` field of the instruction.  
- **Invalid‑input handling** – If the rounded result is not representable in the unsigned 64‑bit destination format, the result is clipped to the nearest representable value and the *invalid* exception flag is set.  
- **Inexact‑input handling** – The *inexact* exception flag is set when the rounded result differs from the original operand value, provided the *invalid* flag is not set.  

**Table 28 – Domains of float‑to‑integer conversions and behavior for invalid inputs (excerpt for FCVT.LU.S)**  

```
+------------------------------+-----------+
|                              | FCVT.LU.S |
+------------------------------+-----------+
| Minimum valid input (after   | 0         |
| rounding)                    |           |
+------------------------------+-----------+
| Maximum valid input (after   |           |
| rounding)                    |           |
+------------------------------+-----------+
| Output for out‑of‑range       | 0         |
| negative input               |           |
+------------------------------+-----------+
| Output for out‑of‑range       |           |
| positive input               |           |
+------------------------------+-----------+
| Output for NaN               |           |
+------------------------------+-----------+
```
======================================================= END - 13 =======================================================

## 21.5. Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

Floating-point-to-integer and integer-to-floating-point conversion instructions are encoded in the OPFP major opcode space. FCVT.W.D or FCVT.L.D converts a double-precision floating-point number in floating-point  register rs1 to  a  signed  32-bit  or  64-bit  integer,  respectively,  in  integer  register rd . FCVT.D.W or FCVT.D.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, in integer register rs1 into  a  double-precision  floating-point  number  in  floating-point  register rd .  FCVT.WU.D,  FCVT.LU.D, FCVT.D.WU,  and  FCVT.D.LU  variants convert to or from unsigned integer values. For  RV64, FCVT.W[U].D sign-extends the 32-bit result. FCVT.L[U].D and FCVT.D.L[U] are RV64-only instructions. The range of valid inputs for FCVT. int .D and the behavior for invalid inputs are the same as for FCVT. int .S.

All  floating-point  to  integer  and  integer  to  floating-point  conversion  instructions  round  according  to the rm field. Note FCVT.D.W[U] always produces an exact result and is unaffected by rounding mode.

<image>

The double-precision to single-precision and single-precision to double-precision conversion instructions,  FCVT.S.D  and  FCVT.D.S,  are  encoded  in  the  OP-FP  major  opcode  space  and  both  the source and destination are floating-point registers. The rs2 field encodes the datatype of the source, and the fmt field encodes the datatype of the destination. FCVT.S.D rounds according to the RM field; FCVT.D.S will never round.

<image>

Floating-point  to  floating-point  sign-injection  instructions,  FSGNJ.D,  FSGNJN.D,  and  FSGNJX.D  are defined analogously to the single-precision sign-injection instruction.

<image>

For  XLEN≥64  only,  instructions  are  provided  to  move  bit  patterns  between  the  floating-point  and integer  registers.  FMV.X.D  moves  the  double-precision  value  in  floating-point  register rs1 to  a

representation in IEEE 754-2008 standard encoding in integer register rd . FMV.D.X moves the doubleprecision  value  encoded  in  IEEE  754-2008  standard  encoding  from  the  integer  register rs1 to  the floating-point register rd .

FMV.X.D  and  FMV.D.X  do  not  modify  the  bits  being  transferred;  in  particular,  the  payloads  of  noncanonical NaNs are preserved.

<image>

<image>

Early versions of the RISC-V ISA had additional instructions to allow RV32 systems to transfer between the upper and lower portions of a 64-bit floating-point register and an integer register.  However,  these  would  be  the  only  instructions  with  partial register writes and would add complexity in implementations with recoded floatingpoint or register renaming, requiring a pipeline read-modify-write sequence. Scaling up  to  handling  quad-precision  for  RV32  and  RV64  would  also  require  additional instructions  if  they  were  to  follow  this  pattern.  The  ISA  was  defined  to  reduce  the number of explicit int-float register moves, by having conversions and comparisons write  results  to  the  appropriate  register  file,  so  we  expect  the  benefit  of  these instructions to be lower than for other ISAs.

We note that for systems that implement a 64-bit floating-point unit including fused multiply-add  support  and  64-bit  floating-point  loads  and  stores,  the  marginal hardware  cost  of  moving  from  a  32-bit  to  a  64-bit  integer  datapath  is  low,  and  a software  ABI  supporting  32-bit  wide  address-space  and  pointers  can  be  used  to avoid growth of static data and dynamic memory traffic.
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
FCVT.S.D – double‑precision to single‑precision floating‑point conversion  

* **Purpose** – Converts a double‑precision floating‑point value in a floating‑point register to a single‑precision floating‑point value, placing the result in a floating‑point register.  

* **Encoding** – Implemented in the OP‑FP major opcode space. Both the source and destination are floating‑point registers. The **rs2** field encodes the datatype of the source operand, and the **fmt** field encodes the datatype of the destination operand.  

* **Rounding** – The conversion rounds according to the **RM** (rounding‑mode) field of the instruction.  

* **Behavioural notes** – The instruction follows the same rounding‑mode semantics as other floating‑point‑to‑floating‑point conversion instructions; it does **not** produce an exact result regardless of rounding mode.  

(Only the information explicitly stated in the source text about FCVT.S.D is captured above.)

The text below:

## 25.3. Round-to-Integer Instructions

The FROUND.S instruction rounds the single-precision floating-point number in floating-point register rs1 to an integer, according to the rounding mode specified in the instruction's rm field. It then writes that integer, represented as a single-precision floating-point number, to floating-point register rd . Zero and  infinite  inputs  are  copied  to rd unmodified.  Signaling  NaN  inputs  cause  the  invalid  operation exception flag to be set; no other exception flags are set. FROUND.S is encoded like FCVT.S.D, but with rs2 =4.

The FROUNDNX.S instruction is defined similarly, but it also sets the inexact exception flag if the input differs from the rounded result and is not NaN. FROUNDNX.S is encoded like FCVT.S.D, but with rs2 =5.

If the D extension is implemented, FROUND.D and FROUNDNX.D instructions are analogously defined to  operate  on  double-precision  numbers.  They  are  encoded  like  FCVT.D.S,  but  with rs2 =4  and  5, respectively,

If  the  Zfh  extension  is  implemented,  FROUND.H  and  FROUNDNX.H  instructions  are  analogously defined to operate on half-precision numbers. They are encoded like FCVT.H.S, but with rs2 =4 and 5, respectively,

If the Q extension is implemented, FROUND.Q and FROUNDNX.Q instructions are analogously defined to  operate  on  quad-precision  numbers.  They  are  encoded  like  FCVT.Q.S,  but  with rs2 =4  and  5, respectively,

<image>

The FROUNDNX.fmt instructions implement the IEEE 754-2019 roundToIntegralExact  operation,  and  the  FROUND.fmt  instructions  implement  the other operations in the roundToIntegral family.
============================================================
=========
RESPONSE:
=========
============================================================
- The FCVT.S.D instruction uses the same binary encoding format as the FROUND.S and FROUNDNX.S instructions, differing only in the value of the **rs2** field.  

- Specifically, the text states:  
  *"FROUND.S is encoded like FCVT.S.D, but with rs2 =4."*  
  *"FROUNDNX.S is encoded like FCVT.S.D, but with rs2 =5."*  

  From this, it follows that the FCVT.S.D instruction is encoded with the same opcode, funct, rd, and rs1 fields as those round‑to‑integer instructions, and its rs2 field takes a value **other than 4 or 5** (the exact value is not given in the excerpt).
======================================================= END - 15 =======================================================
########################################################################################################################
########################################################################################################################
########################################################################################################################

GLOBAL NUMBER: 16
LOCAL NUMBER: 1
NAME: FCVT.S.L
